content.text: What is Software as a Service?

Software as a Service (SaaS) is the cloud model where a vendor delivers a fully managed application over the internet. You do not provision VMs, install runtimes, patch the OS, or manage databases — the provider handles every layer of the stack. You subscribe, log in, and use the software.

SaaS sits at the top of the IaaS → PaaS → SaaS spectrum. Where IaaS gives you VMs and PaaS gives you a managed runtime, SaaS gives you a finished product. The only thing you own is your data and how you configure the application for your organization.
The Microsoft SaaS Ecosystem
Microsoft operates one of the largest SaaS portfolios. The major products most Azure customers encounter are:
Microsoft 365

— Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Outlook, Teams, SharePoint, OneDrive. Licensed per user per month. Microsoft handles datacenter, OS, and application updates. Enterprise customers get SSO via Microsoft Entra ID.
Dynamics 365
— ERP and CRM modules: Sales, Customer Service, Finance, Supply Chain, Commerce. Built on the Power Platform (Power Apps, Power Automate, Power BI).
GitHub
— Source control, Actions CI/CD, Copilot, Advanced Security, Packages, Dependabot. Microsoft acquired GitHub in 2018; it is now deeply integrated with Azure DevOps and Entra ID.
Power BI
— Business intelligence and data visualization. Connects to hundreds of data sources; premium workspace publishes reports accessible via embedding APIs.
Azure DevOps
— Boards, Repos, Pipelines, Test Plans, Artifacts. Hosted service with no infrastructure to manage; licensed per active user per month.
How SaaS Works Internally
Understanding the internal model helps you make better decisions around data, compliance, and integration.
Multi-tenancy

Most SaaS products are multi-tenant: a single application deployment serves many customers simultaneously. Tenants share compute and storage infrastructure but are logically isolated — your data cannot be read by another tenant. Isolation is enforced at the application layer (row-level security, tenant ID in every query) and sometimes at the database layer (separate schemas or databases per tenant).
Subscription licensing

SaaS pricing is almost always subscription-based — per user per month, per seat, or per consumption unit. This converts capital expenditure (buying software licenses) to operating expenditure (recurring subscription). Most enterprise SaaS products offer tiered plans: a free or developer tier, a business tier with more features, and an enterprise tier with SSO, audit logs, compliance certifications, and SLAs.
Automatic updates

The vendor deploys updates transparently. You always use the latest version without planning a maintenance window. This is a major advantage for security patches, but it also means you cannot pin to an older version if a feature change breaks your workflow — vendor SLAs and preview-channel programs are the levers available to enterprise customers.
SaaS Integration Architecture

The typical enterprise pattern: employees authenticate via Entra ID (SSO), and custom Azure applications connect to SaaS products through their APIs. The diagram below shows the reference architecture used in this section.

attrs.alt: SaaS integration architecture: Enterprise Users authenticate via Entra ID SSO, Azure App Service (with managed identity) calls Microsoft 365 via Graph API, Dynamics 365 via REST, and GitHub via REST; webhooks route back through Azure Functions
attrs.title: SaaS Integration Architecture

content.text: Integrating SaaS via Microsoft Graph
Microsoft Graph is the single REST API surface for Microsoft 365, Teams, Entra ID, and more. A single endpoint —
https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0
— exposes users, groups, emails, calendar events, SharePoint files, Teams channels, and Teams messages.
Register an app in Entra ID

# Create an app registration and capture the app (client) ID
az ad app create \
  --display-name "MyGraphApp" \
  --sign-in-audience AzureADMyOrg

# Add Microsoft Graph delegated permission: Mail.Read
az ad app permission add \
  --id <app-id> \
  --api 00000003-0000-0000-c000-000000000000 \
  --api-permissions 570282fd-fa5c-430d-a7fd-fc8dc98a9dca=Scope

# Grant admin consent (required for application permissions in automation)
az ad app permission admin-consent --id <app-id>
Call Graph from Python (daemon / service account)

import httpx
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential, ClientSecretCredential

# For local dev use DefaultAzureCredential (az login / VS Code / environment)
# For production use a managed identity — remove secret entirely
credential = DefaultAzureCredential()

def get_graph_token(credential):
    # Microsoft Graph scope
    token = credential.get_token("https://graph.microsoft.com/.default")
    return token.token

def list_users(credential):
    token = get_graph_token(credential)
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}
    resp = httpx.get(
        "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users",
        headers=headers,
        params={"$select": "displayName,mail,userPrincipalName", "$top": 50}
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()["value"]

users = list_users(credential)
for u in users:
    print(u["displayName"], u["mail"])
Send an email via Graph
Entra ID SSO for SaaS Applications

Enterprise SaaS products support SSO via SAML 2.0 or OpenID Connect. Azure Entra ID is the identity provider; the SaaS application is the service provider (SP) or OIDC relying party. The configuration is done once in the Entra ID Enterprise Applications gallery — no code changes in your Azure workloads.

# Assign a gallery SaaS app to a user group via CLI
# 1. Find the enterprise application object ID
az ad sp list --display-name "Salesforce" --query "[0].id" -o tsv

# 2. Assign the group (all group members get SSO access)
az ad app permission grant \
  --id <enterprise-app-object-id> \
  --api <sp-app-id>

# For more control, use Entra ID Conditional Access policies:
# Entra portal → Protection → Conditional Access → New policy
# Condition: Cloud apps = Salesforce
# Grant: Require MFA + Compliant device
Consuming SaaS Webhooks
Many SaaS products push events to your endpoints rather than requiring polling. Microsoft Graph supports change notifications (webhooks) for mailbox, calendar, Teams, and SharePoint events.

# Register a Graph change notification subscription
def create_subscription(credential, notification_url: str):
    token = get_graph_token(credential)
    headers = {
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {token}",
        "Content-Type": "application/json"
    }
    # Get notified when any user receives a new email
    sub = {
        "changeType": "created",
        "notificationUrl": notification_url,  # your Azure Function HTTPS endpoint
        "resource": "/users/noreply@contoso.com/messages",
        "expirationDateTime": "2026-07-15T00:00:00Z",
        "clientState": "my-secret-state-token"  # validate in your handler
    }
    resp = httpx.post(
        "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/subscriptions",
        headers=headers,
        json=sub
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()["id"]
The notification endpoint (your Azure Function) must respond to a validation handshake on subscription creation (Graph sends a
validationToken
query param and expects it echoed back as plain text with HTTP 200), then process event payloads for each subsequent notification.
GitHub API Integration

GitHub is a SaaS product with a rich REST and GraphQL API. The recommended authentication method for server-to-server calls is a GitHub App (fine-grained, installable per-organization) rather than a personal access token.
Benefits of SaaS
Zero infrastructure to manage.
No VMs, no OS patches, no database backups, no capacity planning for the application layer.
Always current.
Security patches and features roll out automatically. You cannot fall behind on updates (a double-edged sword).
Built-in high availability.
Microsoft 365 publishes a 99.9% uptime SLA for most services. You inherit HA without designing for it.
Rapid deployment.
Provision a new Microsoft 365 tenant and add users in minutes. No procurement, no installation, no configuration of server roles.
Rich integration surface.
Microsoft Graph, GitHub REST/GraphQL, Dynamics Web API, and Power Platform connectors let you build workflows that span multiple SaaS products.
Trade-offs and Limitations
No core customization.

You cannot change the application's core logic. You can only configure what the vendor exposes. If the product does not do what you need, your options are workarounds, third-party add-ins, or switching products.
Data sovereignty constraints.

Your data lives in the vendor's infrastructure. Multi-geo controls exist for Microsoft 365 (data residency commitments by country), but you must verify which regions store your data and confirm compliance with regulations like GDPR or local data protection laws.
Vendor lock-in.

If you deeply integrate Teams, SharePoint, and Power Automate, migrating to a competing platform is expensive. Mitigate by using standard protocols (OIDC, SCIM, REST) where possible and keeping integration logic in your own code.
Per-seat cost at scale.
At thousands of users, per-seat SaaS licensing can exceed the cost of building an equivalent hosted PaaS application. Run a total cost of ownership (TCO) analysis before committing.
Limited observability.
You cannot instrument the vendor's application. You get audit logs and usage analytics through the vendor's admin portal, not your own Application Insights dashboards.
SaaS vs PaaS vs Build: Decision Guide
Scenario
Recommendation
Rationale
Email, calendar, collaboration
SaaS (Microsoft 365)
Solved commodity problem; do not build
CRM for standard sales process
SaaS (Dynamics 365 / Salesforce)
Standard workflows; configure, don't code
Custom business logic web app
PaaS (App Service + SQL)
SaaS won't fit; PaaS avoids managing VMs
High-performance ML training at scale
IaaS (GPU VMs)
Need low-level control over CUDA, MPI, topology
Source control and CI
SaaS (GitHub / Azure DevOps)
No competitive advantage in self-hosting Git
Security and Compliance
Shared responsibility in SaaS
In SaaS, the vendor owns everything from the datacenter through the application layer. Your responsibility is narrower but still significant:
Who has access to the application (user provisioning, group membership, licensing)
How data enters and leaves (DLP policies, external sharing settings, API permissions)
Conditional Access policies (MFA, compliant device, named location restrictions)
Classification and labeling of sensitive data stored in SharePoint and Teams
Reviewing audit logs for suspicious access patterns (Entra ID Sign-in logs, Microsoft Purview Audit)
Data Loss Prevention (DLP)

Microsoft Purview DLP policies scan content in Exchange, SharePoint, OneDrive, and Teams for sensitive information types (credit card numbers, national IDs, HIPAA data). A policy can block sharing, apply encryption, or alert the security team — all without touching your application code.
Common Mistakes
Storing secrets in SaaS tools.
Pasting API keys or connection strings into Teams messages or SharePoint documents. They should be in Azure Key Vault; references go into environment variables.
Using personal access tokens instead of apps.
PATs are tied to a user account; if the user leaves, the integration breaks. Use service principals or GitHub Apps.
Granting over-broad API permissions.
Requesting
Mail.ReadWrite
when you only need
Mail.Send
. Graph permissions are granular — request exactly what the integration requires.
Ignoring webhook subscription expiry.
Graph change notification subscriptions expire (max 3 days for mail, 60 minutes for Teams). Build a renewal job that runs before expiry to avoid silent notification gaps.
Not validating webhook payloads.
Any attacker who knows your webhook URL can POST fake events. Always validate the
clientState
token in each notification before processing.
Best Practices
Use managed identities or GitHub Apps for service-to-service calls — no secrets to rotate or accidentally commit.
Scope API permissions to the minimum required. Review them during access reviews and remove unused permissions.
Enable Entra ID Conditional Access for every SaaS application. Require MFA and compliant devices for access to business-critical data.
Automate user lifecycle (SCIM provisioning) so accounts are deactivated the moment an employee leaves — do not rely on manual offboarding.
Monitor Graph API throttling: each app has per-tenant rate limits. Implement exponential backoff and check the
Retry-After
header on 429 responses.
Export SaaS audit logs to Azure Monitor or Microsoft Sentinel for long-term retention and SIEM correlation — the native admin portal logs often have 90-day limits.
Operational Considerations
Monitoring SaaS dependencies
Your application's availability can be coupled to the SaaS provider's uptime. Track dependencies explicitly:
Subscribe to the Microsoft 365 Service Health dashboard (admin.microsoft.com → Service health) and Microsoft Azure Status (status.azure.com) for incident notifications.
Set up synthetic monitors in Application Insights that call your Graph integration endpoints on a schedule — you will see failures before users report them.
Implement circuit-breaker logic in your integration code. If Graph returns 5xx errors for three consecutive calls, stop retrying for 60 seconds to avoid thundering-herd amplification.
Cost management

SaaS cost is primarily license quantity × price. Review license assignments monthly — unused seats are pure waste. Use Entra ID's license assignment reports to find users who have not signed in for 90+ days and reclaim their licenses.
Mental Model

Think of SaaS like renting a fully-furnished apartment rather than buying land and building a house. You move in, arrange the furniture within the unit, and call maintenance when something breaks — but you cannot knock down walls or change the plumbing. The landlord (vendor) handles repairs, security, and building upgrades. Your job is to use the space wisely and follow the house rules.
Summary
SaaS delivers complete applications over the internet — no infrastructure or runtime management required from the customer.
The Microsoft SaaS portfolio (Microsoft 365, Dynamics 365, GitHub, Power BI, Azure DevOps) is the most common SaaS layer in Azure-centric organizations.
Integrate SaaS products via Microsoft Graph (REST/SDK), standard OAuth2/OIDC (SSO), SCIM (provisioning), and webhooks (change notifications).
Security responsibility shifts to identity, access control, DLP policies, and audit log monitoring — not infrastructure.
Choose SaaS for solved commodity problems (email, CRM, CI). Use PaaS or IaaS when you need custom logic, deep customization, or control over your data plane.
Avoid personal access tokens, over-broad Graph permissions, and missing webhook validation — the most common integration mistakes.
